description: Quality rules for poker, blackjack, solitaire, and other card games.

guidelines.rule: The human player MUST have explicit controls for their turn — Fold, Call, Raise (or Hit, Stand for blackjack). The game MUST pause and wait for the human's input. AI opponents play automatically AFTER the human acts.
guidelines.why: This is the single most common bug: the game plays all players automatically with no human interaction. Every card game session has required this fix.
examples: Show Fold/Call/Raise buttons during human's turn
Disable buttons during AI turns
Show 'Your Turn' indicator prominently

guidelines.rule: Cards that should be hidden (opponents' hole cards, undealt cards) MUST show card back graphics, not be invisible or show their face values. Use a card back SVG pattern.
guidelines.why: Games frequently show all cards face-up or make hidden cards invisible, breaking the game mechanics.
examples: Opponents' hole cards show card back until showdown
Deck shows card back for undealt cards

guidelines.rule: Use proper card face rendering with rank and suit symbols — never plain text like '10H' or 'AS'. Use SVG card faces with corner indices and centered suit pips.
guidelines.why: Plain text card representations look amateur. Proper card graphics are essential for a polished look.
examples: Use renderCard('A', '♠') if the asset pack provides it
Show rank in top-left and bottom-right corners
Red suits (♥♦) in red, black suits (♠♣) in black

guidelines.rule: Implement complete hand evaluation with proper ranking. For poker: Royal Flush > Straight Flush > Four of a Kind > Full House > Flush > Straight > Three of a Kind > Two Pair > One Pair > High Card.
guidelines.why: Incorrect hand evaluation makes the game unplayable. Users notice when a pair beats a flush.
examples: Evaluate all 5-card combinations from 7 cards (Texas Hold'em)
Handle kicker comparisons for tie-breaking

guidelines.rule: Handle chip/money management correctly: track each player's stack, deduct bets properly, handle all-in scenarios (side pots), and eliminate players who run out of chips.
guidelines.why: Money bugs break game balance — players getting free chips, bets not deducting, or eliminated players still acting.
examples: Player can't bet more than their stack
All-in creates a side pot
Busted player is removed from future hands

guidelines.rule: Shuffle the deck properly before each hand. Use Fisher-Yates shuffle. Never reuse cards within a hand.
guidelines.why: Duplicate cards or predictable dealing patterns break game integrity.
examples: Create fresh 52-card deck each hand
Fisher-Yates: for i from n-1 to 1, swap arr[i] with arr[random 0..i]

guidelines.rule: Show the pot/stakes clearly at all times. Players should always see how much is in the pot and what the current bet is.
guidelines.why: Players can't make informed betting decisions without seeing the pot size and current bet.
examples: Pot: $45 displayed prominently
Current bet: $10 shown near controls
Each player's chip stack visible

guidelines.rule: Use a casino-style felt green table background with proper card spacing. Position players around the table (bottom = human, top/sides = AI). Felt green range: #1e6b3a to #2a7d4a, with a subtle radial gradient (darker edges, lighter center) OR a subtle noise texture to evoke felt fabric. Do NOT ship a flat-gray or solid-black background for card games.
guidelines.why: A green felt table is the universal visual language for card games. Multiple generations shipped with flat dark backgrounds (#1a1a2e etc.) and looked like prototypes, not card games. User feedback: 'the play area would probably look better with some kind of felt table (green or something)'.
examples: Radial gradient green felt background
Human player at bottom center
Community cards in center of table

guidelines.rule: VISIBILITY RULE — THE ONE YOU CANNOT GET WRONG: The human player is the ONLY seat whose cards are rendered FACE-UP showing full rank and suit. Every other player (AI1, AI2, AI3) shows FACE-DOWN card backs — never face-up, never visible, never hinted. The human's cards must render at the BOTTOM of the screen with rank + suit visible at minimum 40px height per card. NEVER apply card-back rendering to the human's hand. NEVER render the human's cards as blue rectangles, generic rectangles, or anything that hides the rank/suit. If you find yourself writing `.hand .card { background: #1a3a8a; }` for the human zone — STOP, you are rendering a card back instead of a card face.
guidelines.why: The Hearts mini-app generator has repeatedly rendered ALL four players' cards as blue rectangles / card backs, including the human's, making the game literally unplayable. This happens because rule cg-002 (hide opponents' cards) gets applied uniformly to all seats instead of ONLY to AI seats. The fix is to make the human/AI distinction unmissable: the human is special, every other seat uses card-back graphics. If any seat's cards are indistinguishable from another seat's, this rule is being violated.
examples: GOOD: .human .card { background:#fff; color:#000; border-radius:6px; width:60px; height:88px; } and inside each card: A♠
GOOD: .ai .card-back { background: repeating-linear-gradient(45deg,#1a3a8a 0 8px,#2c4fa8 8px 16px); width:38px; height:54px; } — card-back pattern ONLY on .ai
BAD: .card-back { ... } applied to .human .card — the human MUST show faces
BAD: rendering 13 blue rectangles at the bottom with no rank text — those are card backs, the human's seat MUST NOT use card backs
Test you can apply mentally: 'If I take a screenshot right now, can I tell what rank + suit each of my 13 cards is?' If the answer is NO, cg-009 is being violated

guidelines.rule: CARD BACK USAGE MATRIX — where to apply a card-back graphic vs a card-face graphic: (1) The human player's hand: ALWAYS card-face (rank + suit visible). NEVER card-back. (2) AI player zones (AI1, AI2, AI3, CPU, North/East/West, etc.): ALWAYS card-back. NEVER card-face until the card is played into the trick. (3) The deck / stock pile: card-back. (4) Cards played into the trick (center area): card-face — once a card leaves a hand into the trick, everyone sees it. (5) End-of-hand / showdown: card-face for all — all hands revealed. This matrix covers every card location; if you're unsure which to render, re-read this list.
guidelines.why: Every Hearts / Spades / Poker / Rummy generation has gotten at least one cell of this matrix wrong — most commonly (1) rendering the human's hand as card backs. Spelling out all five cells in a single rule removes ambiguity.
examples: function renderCard(card, zone) { if (zone === 'human' || zone === 'trick' || zone === 'showdown') return faceHTML(card); else return backHTML(); }

NEVER: `
` inside `.human-hand` — that's the wrong cell of the matrix
Positioning check: if your layout has 13 cards at `bottom: 2%` and they're all card backs, the matrix is wrong

guidelines.rule: Table layout: use position:absolute with percentage-based positioning inside a 100vh container. Human at bottom:8%, AI opponents distributed at top:5% and sides at top:35%. Community cards at top:42% center. Pot display at center. Buttons below human's cards.
guidelines.why: Every poker game iteration has had layout issues — players crammed at the top, empty space at the bottom, or elements off-screen. This specific layout works.
examples: #table { width:100%; height:100vh; position:relative; overflow:hidden; }
.human { position:absolute; bottom:8%; left:50%; transform:translateX(-50%); }
.ai-top { position:absolute; top:5%; left:50%; transform:translateX(-50%); }
.ai-left { position:absolute; left:5%; top:35%; }
.ai-right { position:absolute; right:5%; top:35%; }
.community { position:absolute; top:42%; left:50%; transform:translate(-50%,-50%); }

guidelines.rule: For trick-taking games (Hearts, Spades, Bridge, Euchre) use a 4-seat cross layout: human 'You' at BOTTOM, one AI at TOP, one AI on LEFT, one AI on RIGHT. Each AI zone shows a small stack of card backs plus a card count badge. Trick area in the CENTER showing 4 played cards in a cross pattern (played card from each seat slides toward center). Human's hand is a fanned row of face-up cards at the very bottom, SORTED by suit then rank, with cards larger than AI card backs (80x112 vs 38x54). CRITICAL: use height:100vh on the game container, NEVER min-height:100vh, and never use flex:1 on the table element without a fixed parent height — it will collapse and pile everything at the top.
guidelines.why: Hearts mini-app came out with all 4 player zones piled at the top because the flex chain collapsed when body used min-height and .table used flex:1. The trick area was empty and unusable.
examples: body { height:100vh; margin:0; overflow:hidden; }
#table { position:relative; width:100%; height:100vh; } (NOT flex:1)
#zoneS { position:absolute; bottom:2%; left:50%; transform:translateX(-50%); } — Human
#zoneN { position:absolute; top:2%; left:50%; transform:translateX(-50%); } — AI top
#zoneW { position:absolute; left:2%; top:50%; transform:translateY(-50%); } — AI left
#zoneE { position:absolute; right:2%; top:50%; transform:translateY(-50%); } — AI right
#trick { position:absolute; top:50%; left:50%; transform:translate(-50%,-50%); width:200px; height:200px; } — center cross
Sort human hand: hand.sort((a,b) => suitOrder[a.s] - suitOrder[b.s] || rankOrder[a.r] - rankOrder[b.r]);

guidelines.rule: Playing cards MUST be readable at all times. Always use a WHITE card face with BLACK text for spades/clubs and RED text for hearts/diamonds. Never render cards with a dark background. Card faces must stay white even in dark-mode themes. Use `background:#fff; color:#000;` on .card and override color:#c0392b (or similar red) for .hearts/.diamonds.
guidelines.why: Hearts mini-app rendered played cards with a black/dark background making the rank and suit unreadable. Users could not tell what cards were in the trick. Dark-mode CSS was incorrectly applied to the card face instead of just the table background.
examples: GOOD: .card { background:#fff; color:#000; } .card.hearts, .card.diamonds { color:#c0392b; }
BAD: .card { background:var(--card-bg); } where --card-bg flips dark in dark mode
BAD: @media (prefers-color-scheme:dark) { .card { background:#1a1a1a; color:#eee; } }
Card back: use a dark pattern background, but FRONT must be white always

guidelines.rule: Sort the human player's hand by SUIT first, then by RANK within each suit, using alternating-color suit order: clubs (black), diamonds (red), spades (black), hearts (red). This puts same-color suits apart so the eye can easily spot cards. Always sort AFTER receiving cards and AFTER every play.
guidelines.why: Unsorted hands are painful — the user has to scan every card to find what they want to play. Trick-taking games like Hearts and Bridge REQUIRE suit-grouped sorting so the player can see voids and plan the trick.
examples: hand.sort((a,b) => suitOrder[a.suit]-suitOrder[b.suit] || rankOrder[a.rank]-rankOrder[b.rank]);
For Hearts specifically: always use the alternating-color order above

guidelines.rule: Cribbage pegging phase MUST support a Go button path: when a player CANNOT play a card without exceeding 31, they click a 'Go' (pass) button — NOT a silent auto-skip. The LAST PLAYER TO LAY A CARD before everyone passes or someone hits exactly 31 scores 1 point (for 'Go') or 2 (for exactly 31), then the running count resets to 0 and play continues from that player's opponent using the REMAINING cards in each hand. Score 2 for reaching exactly 15 or 31, 2 for a pair, 6 for three-of-a-kind, 12 for four-of-a-kind, and 1 per card for a run of 3+ in any order. The running count and each score event must be shown in real time. Pegging continues until BOTH hands are fully played out (each player played all 4 cards).
guidelines.why: Every Cribbage generation so far has shipped one of two bugs: (a) no visible Go button, so when the player can't legally play, the game appears stuck; (b) Go awarded to 'the opponent', which is backwards — Go goes to the LAST PLAYER who laid a card. Also: implementations often reset on 31 but don't continue to play out the remaining cards, ending a round with players still holding cards.
examples: UI: show a 'Go' button that enables ONLY when player has cards but none can be played under 31
Logic: if playerGoSaid && cpuGoSaid → lastPlayerToLay gets 1 point, runningCount=0, pegging continues with lastPlayerToLay's opponent
Exactly 31: player who laid the card reaching 31 gets 2 points, runningCount=0, continue with remaining cards
Continue until BOTH hands empty — don't stop after the first reset
After a reset: clear or visually fade the played-cards display for that sub-round; the NEW sub-round starts with an empty play area
Last-card bonus: if the final card of the hand is laid without exceeding 31 (but isn't exactly 31), that player gets 1 point for 'last card'

guidelines.rule: Cribbage hand scoring MUST count ALL scoring combinations: fifteens (any subset summing to 15 = 2 pts each), pairs/pairs-royal/double-pairs-royal, runs of 3/4/5, flushes (4+ in hand, 5 with starter for crib), nobs (J matching starter suit = 1 pt). Use the starter card (cut card) for all hand counts. The crib is scored by the dealer only.
guidelines.why: Hand scoring in cribbage is combinatorially complex — a hand of 5-5-5-J with a 5 starter scores 29 points (maximum). Implementations that only count pairs and simple fifteens will score most hands wrong.
examples: Enumerate ALL subsets of 5 cards (hand + starter) for fifteen counting
Runs: find longest run first; a double run (3-4-4-5) scores as two runs of 3 = 6 pts
Flush: 4 cards same suit in hand = 4 pts (NOT crib); 5 including starter = 5 pts
Nobs: J in hand matching starter's suit = 1 pt; Nibs/his-heels = dealer scores 2 if cut card is J
Always show the breakdown: 'Two fifteens: 4, Pair: 2, Run of 3: 3 = 9 points'

guidelines.rule: Cribbage board MUST use a warm wood/brown aesthetic — brown background (e.g. #6b4423, #8b5a2b gradient), dark-wood track lanes, and pegs rendered as small circles in two CONTRASTING colors (e.g. one yellow/gold, one red). The board is the classic 120-hole two-track layout grouped in fives (gap every 5 holes so you can count at a glance). Each player has two pegs: FRONT peg = current score, BACK peg = previous score; scoring a point LEAPFROGS the back peg to (front position + points_scored). Win at 121. Show the numeric score alongside the board.
guidelines.why: Earlier generations rendered the peg board on a dark-blue/gray background with uniform-colored peg dots — looked like a generic progress bar, not a cribbage board. The wood brown + 5-hole groupings + leapfrog pegs are what make it READ as cribbage to the player.
examples: background: linear-gradient(135deg, #8b5a2b, #6b4423); (warm wood tones)
Track: two parallel rows of 60 holes, rendered as dark brown recessed dots; gap every 5 holes
Player 1 pegs: #f1c40f (gold); Player 2 pegs: #c0392b (red). No yellow-on-yellow peg/background.
Front/back pegs visually distinct (front = solid, back = slightly smaller or lighter)
Text labels: 'YOU 47' and 'CPU 43' near the board
Animate peg leapfrog: back peg jumps forward to (front + delta) in a smooth 300ms transition

guidelines.rule: Each Cribbage round MUST prominently display WHOSE crib it is this round using an EXPLICIT LABEL containing the words 'Your Crib' or 'CPU's Crib' (or '\'s Crib' for the opponent's name). A 'DEALER: You' / 'DEALER: CPU' indicator ALONE is INSUFFICIENT — casual players don't know dealer == crib owner. The label MUST be a separate persistent header element (e.g. 'Round 3 — CPU's Crib' or a pill near the crib pile). The dealer alternates every round and the crib always belongs to the current dealer.
guidelines.why: User confirmed feedback: 'Is this my crib or the computers crib this round?' — they couldn't tell. Without this indicator, the player doesn't know whose counting comes last, can't strategize their discards correctly (throw trash to opponent's crib, throw helpers to your own), and feels confused throughout the hand. Phase 2026-05-11 strengthening: User reported a generation that showed 'DEALER: You' but still felt ambiguous — the label needs to literally contain the word 'Crib' or it doesn't register.
examples: Header shows 'Round 3 — CPU's crib' or 'Round 4 — Your crib'
Crib card pile has a small tag above it: 'YOUR CRIB' (gold) or 'CPU CRIB' (red)
Tooltip on the crib pile: 'The dealer counts the crib last. This round: You are dealer.'
At round start, briefly animate a 'Dealer: YOU' badge near the score
Discard phase instruction text names the crib owner explicitly: 'Pick 2 to discard to CPU's crib' or 'to your crib'

guidelines.rule: Cribbage MUST use consistent terminology across UI strings AND code: ROUND = one complete deal-play-count cycle (ends when both hands + crib are counted). HAND = the 4 cards held by a player (the 'show' count source). PEGGING PLAY = the sub-phase of alternating card plays up to 31. 31-RUN (or 'run to 31') = one sub-sequence of the pegging play that ends on a Go or exactly 31. THE SHOW = the end-of-round counting phase. CRIB = dealer's bonus 4-card hand counted last. GO = pass when you can't play under 31. PEG = a point scored. Do NOT use 'hand' to mean 'round' in UI strings.
guidelines.why: When the LLM conflates 'hand' and 'round' and 'play', the UI says 'Play your hand' when it means 'play ONE card of your hand', 'End of hand' when it means 'end of round', etc. Users get confused and the auto-fix agent's instructions become ambiguous. Pinning the vocabulary gives every downstream rule a shared glossary.
examples: Status: 'Round 3 of ~6 — Pegging play — Running count: 23'
Buttons: 'Play card', 'Go' (NOT 'Pass hand')
End-of-round modal: 'Round 3 show: Your hand 8 · CPU hand 6 · Your crib 4'
Instruction: 'Select 2 cards from your hand to discard to the crib' (NOT 'discard from your round')
Internally in code: `currentRound`, `pegHistory`, `playerHand`, `crib`, `starterCard` — one term per concept

guidelines.rule: During the pegging play, ALL cards played since the last reset (31 or go) MUST stay visibly face-up in a dedicated 'play area' row until that 31-run ends. When the run resets, slide the played cards into a 'previous tricks' zone (or fade them) and start a fresh play area. The current running total goes next to the play area, not alone.
guidelines.why: User feedback: 'Can't see the cards played in a given round to 31' — the existing Cribbage generation hid played cards behind card backs immediately after the play animation, so the player couldn't reason about what had been played. In real cribbage you can see every card in the current play sequence. Hiding them defeats the strategic decision of what to play next.
examples: Play area row with face-up cards in play order: [5♥] [8♠] [3♦] [Total: 16]
On 31 or Go: fade these cards to 30% opacity or slide them out to a 'past plays' stack, then clear for the next sub-run
Running total rendered RIGHT NEXT to the play area: 'Count: 16 / 31'
Each played card should show WHO played it (small colored border: gold for player, red for CPU)
Do NOT flip played cards back to card-back immediately — they stay face-up until reset

guidelines.rule: Every game MUST present a VISIBLE on-screen restart/play-again button when the game ends (game-over, you-win, level-complete). Do NOT rely solely on a menu shortcut like Cmd+N or 'Game > New Game' — users don't discover those mid-play. The restart UI must: (a) appear automatically when the terminal phase is reached, (b) be visually distinct (colored button or large tappable area), (c) clearly say 'Restart' / 'Play Again' / 'New Game' (not just 'OK' or unlabeled), (d) when tapped, fully reset all game state (score, lives, level, score-multiplier, fuel, terrain — anything that accumulated during play). Keep the menu-bar shortcut too (e.g. Cmd+N), but the on-screen button is the primary affordance.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-04 on shipped Lunar Lander: 'There is no restart button for the game: That should be a fundamental wisdom rule to all games if we don't have that already.' The shipped Lunar Lander rendered 'GAME OVER score=0' as static HUD text with no UI affordance. Users had to discover Cmd+N from the menu bar. Same pattern was hit by earlier shipped Pong, BurgerTime, Joust — they all have the menu shortcut but lack a tappable on-screen restart. This is the single most common 'it's a great game but UX feels rough' complaint.
examples: RIGHT (SwiftUI overlay): When phase becomes .gameOver, render `VStack { Text('GAME OVER').font(.largeTitle); Text('Score: \(score)'); Button('Play Again') { restart() }.buttonStyle(.borderedProminent) }.padding(20).background(...).overlay(...)`
RIGHT (SpriteKit overlay): On game-over, add an SKShapeNode panel with 'Game Over' label + an SKLabelNode 'Tap to Restart' that the scene's touchesBegan/mouseDown handler responds to.
RIGHT (HTML game): On game-over, show a centered modal with 'Play Again' button. The modal is dismissable only by clicking the button (don't auto-dismiss).
WRONG: Showing 'GAME OVER score=0' in the HUD label and stopping the game loop. User can't restart without quitting and relaunching.
WRONG: Only Cmd+N / menu shortcut. Users don't think to look there mid-play.
Bonus: keyboard shortcut on the button itself (e.g. Space or Return = Play Again) for users who prefer keyboard.

guidelines.rule: Game backgrounds MUST be warm/atmospheric — radial-gradient or layered linear-gradient with at least one color highlight per corner. NOT pure black, NOT plain solid color. The web Candy Match reference uses a layered gradient: base purple (#1f1026) + radial yellow glow at top-left + radial cyan glow at top-right. Same pattern works for any game: pick a primary color matching the game's theme (purple for candy, dark blue for space, dark green for forest), add 1-2 radial corner glows in complementary colors. SwiftUI: use ZStack { Color + RadialGradient + RadialGradient }.ignoresSafeArea(). The warmth makes the game feel like a finished product instead of a wireframe.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-04 on the catalog Match3: 'The black background is lame'. Switched to layered radial-gradient (purple base + yellow + cyan corners) per the web Candy Match reference. The atmosphere is the difference between a play-test prototype and a polished app — and it costs ~12 lines of SwiftUI to add. Apply broadly: arcade games, board games, card games, puzzles all benefit.
examples: RIGHT (SwiftUI): ZStack { Color(red: 0.12, green: 0.06, blue: 0.15).ignoresSafeArea(); RadialGradient(gradient: Gradient(colors: [Color.yellow.opacity(0.22), .clear]), center: UnitPoint(x: 0.2, y: 0.1), startRadius: 0, endRadius: 360); RadialGradient(...) }

RIGHT (HTML/CSS): background: radial-gradient(circle at 20% 10%, rgba(250,204,21,0.22), transparent 28rem), radial-gradient(circle at 80% 0%, rgba(34,211,238,0.16), transparent 26rem), linear-gradient(145deg, #170b1c 0%, #1f1026 48%, #35141d 100%);
WRONG: backgroundColor = .black, .ignoresSafeArea(), nothing else. Reads like a debug build.
Board game color picks: chess = warm beige + dark walnut accents; checkers = forest green felt + tan corner glow; poker = dark green felt + brass corner highlights.

guidelines.rule: Every clickable/interactive tile in a game (candy in match-3, card in solitaire, key on a piano keyboard, etc.) MUST have: (a) a vertical or 145° linear-gradient fill, NOT a flat color. (b) An inner highlight — a rotated semi-transparent white blob or arc, inset ~12% from the edges, that gives the tile a 3D / glossy appearance. (c) A subtle 1.5pt white-with-low-opacity stroke for definition. (d) A drop-shadow whose color is the tile's BOTTOM gradient color so the tile feels grounded. The web Candy Match reference does this in CSS with `.tile::before { inset: 12%; background: rgba(255,255,255,0.22); transform: rotate(18deg) }`. SwiftUI equivalent: ZStack { RoundedRectangle.fill(LinearGradient(top, bottom)); RoundedRectangle.fill(.white.opacity(0.22)).scaleEffect(0.62).rotationEffect(.degrees(-18)); RoundedRectangle.stroke(.white.opacity(0.28), lineWidth: 1.5) }.shadow(color: bottomColor.opacity(0.5), radius: 6, y: 4).
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-04 on the catalog Match3: 'The jewels look kind of lame, the graphics are very flat and boring.' Adding the gradient + inner shine + colored shadow makes a flat shape look like a candy-shop jewel without bundling any art assets. Same trick works for cards (gradient front + back), keys (white-to-cream gradient + shine), pieces (color-toned gradient + shadow).
examples: RIGHT (Match3 candy): see /Users/lawrencenorman/mystuff/src/mchatai_platform/mchatai-source/frameworks/macos-components/examples/match3/ContentView.swift (function candyTileBody) — gradient + inner shine + stroke + colored shadow
RIGHT (web Candy Match): see frameworks/web-components/examples/candy-match/candy-match.css — .tile + .tile::before + .tile.kind-N gradient classes
WRONG: RoundedRectangle.fill(Color.red) + Image(systemName: 'sparkle') with no gradient, no shine, no shadow — reads as a wireframe placeholder
Reskin guidance: when the user asks for a different theme (e.g. 'space-themed match-3'), keep the gradient+shine+stroke STRUCTURE, only swap the gradient color pairs.

guidelines.rule: Every game state transition that the player triggers MUST have a visible animation, not a snap-cut. Specifically: (1) Tile/piece moves: spring-animation (response: 0.25-0.35, dampingFraction: 0.7) with offset interpolation. (2) Tile-swap (match-3, sliding puzzles): both tiles slide simultaneously toward each other, then settle. (3) Tile/piece removal: scale to 1.3× + fade-out (.easeOut, 0.18s). (4) Refill / collapse (match-3, tetris): new tiles slide DOWN from above using SwiftUI .transition(.asymmetric(insertion: .move(edge: .top).combined(with: .opacity), removal: .opacity)). (5) Cascade chain: stagger animations by 0.20-0.25s per chain pass so the player can see each step. (6) Score increment: animate with .contentTransition(.numericText()) or a brief scale-pulse on the score number. NEVER use snap-cuts on user-initiated state changes — even 0.1s of motion is the difference between 'game' and 'spreadsheet'.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-04 on the catalog Match3: 'the animations are still pretty lame, the jewels don't slide down, there is no pulsing hints, nothing.' Borrowing the web Candy Match's animation choreography (swap glide + pop+fade match + slide-from-top refill) made the game feel alive instead of teleporty. Apply broadly: chess piece moves should glide, card flips should rotate, hop-games should arc, everything you click should respond visibly.
examples: RIGHT (swap glide): withAnimation(.easeInOut(duration: 0.18)) { tileOffsets[a] = ...; tileOffsets[b] = ... }; DispatchQueue.main.asyncAfter(deadline: .now() + 0.20) { engine.swap(a, b); tileOffsets[a] = .zero; tileOffsets[b] = .zero }
RIGHT (pop fade): withAnimation(.easeOut(duration: 0.20)) { poppingTiles.formUnion(matched); ... } -> tile body uses .scaleEffect(isPopping ? 1.3 : 1.0).opacity(isPopping ? 0 : 1)
RIGHT (cascade stagger): for (i, event) in events.enumerated() { DispatchQueue.main.asyncAfter(deadline: .now() + Double(i) * 0.24) { ... } }
WRONG: engine.swap(a, b); engine.findMatches(); engine.collapse(); engine.refill() — all in one synchronous frame; the user sees the board jump from old state to new state with zero feedback.
Anti-pattern: rendering NEW tiles in their final position. They should slide IN from off-screen / above. Use transitions: .move(edge: .top).combined(with: .opacity).

guidelines.rule: After ~5 seconds of player idleness in a game with valid-move gameplay (match-3, sudoku, mahjong-tile-matching, etc.), the game MUST surface a hint by visibly pulsing one valid move. The hint should: (a) compute a real valid move via the engine's findPossibleSwap / findPossibleMove API, (b) animate the involved tiles with a soft scale (1.05x) + glow pulse on a 1s ease-in-out cycle, (c) cancel the hint as soon as the player makes any move. This is the difference between 'I'm stuck' and 'this game wants me to figure it out alone.' Cap idle threshold at 5-8s — any longer feels like punishment.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-04 on the catalog Match3: 'no pulsing hints, nothing'. The web Candy Match doesn't have this either, but it's a UX standard for tile-swap puzzlers. Added to Match3 with a Timer.publish(every: 1) ticker + lastMoveAt tracking + findPossibleSwap helper. Same pattern works for Mahjong (highlight a matchable pair), Sudoku (highlight a cell with only one valid digit), Solitaire (highlight a moveable card).
examples: RIGHT (idle threshold + scale-pulse): @State var lastMoveAt = Date(); Timer.publish(every: 1) { if Date.now - lastMoveAt > 5 { hintPair = engine.findPossibleSwap(); withAnimation(...) { hintPulse.toggle() } } }
RIGHT (cancel on move): when the player commits any swap/move, set hintPair = nil; lastMoveAt = Date()
WRONG: showing the hint immediately after every move (turns it into a tutorial mode the player can't disable)
WRONG: never showing a hint (advanced players don't need it, but new players quit when they're stuck and don't realize valid moves exist)

guidelines.rule: Falling tiles in tile-based games (match-3, Tetris, sliding-tile, Bejeweled, Threes) MUST deform: stretch vertically (sx≈0.92, sy≈1.12) while falling, squash horizontally (sx≈1.18, sy≈0.86) for ~130ms when they land. This is principle #1 of the 12 Disney principles (squash-and-stretch) — the difference between 'graphic moves' and 'candy lands.' Tiles that scale uniformly or just translate feel weightless and stickered onto the board. The squash is volume-preserving (sx*sy≈1.0).
guidelines.why: Real Candy Crush production animations validated 2026-05-04 via web research + Match3 catalog reference rebuild (v6). Without squash-stretch the user said 'they feel like pngs that are just being animated.' With it the tiles read as having weight.
examples: RIGHT: phaseScales(.falling) returns (0.92, 1.12); phaseScales(.squashing) returns (1.18, 0.86); driven by AnimationIntensity.fallStretch and .squashAmount.
RIGHT (with AnimationIntensity): use the `effects.animation-intensity` Lego component — one struct field controls both stretch and squash, presets handle game pace.
WRONG: tiles use a uniform scale (1.0,1.0) the entire fall; reads as weightless icons sliding.

guidelines.rule: Match clears (or any 'item disappears with feedback' moment) MUST bulge before they shrink — anticipation (12 principles #4) + follow-through (#5). Sequence: (a) scale up 1.20–1.30× over 60–100ms with easeOut, (b) shrink to 0 + fade with easeIn over 80–110ms. A flat fade (just opacity 1→0) feels mechanical; bulge-shrink feels satisfying. The eye registers 'something is about to happen' (bulge) BEFORE the disappearance (shrink). The whole motion takes ~180ms.
guidelines.why: v3/v4/v5 of the catalog Match3 used flat fades and the user reported 'animations are lacking, feels flat.' v6 with bulge-shrink read as 'satisfying pop.' Universal animation principle — applies to card flips, item pickups, balloon pops, any binary disappear event.
examples: RIGHT: withAnimation(.easeOut(duration: 0.08)) { phase = .bulging }; sleep 80ms; withAnimation(.easeIn(duration: 0.10)) { phase = .shrinking }; commit clear after shrink.
RIGHT (with AnimationIntensity): bulgeAmount=0.25, bulgeDuration=0.08, shrinkDuration=0.11 are the standard preset.
WRONG: withAnimation(.easeOut) { tile.opacity = 0; tile.scale = 0 }  — single-phase fade-shrink, no anticipation, feels mechanical.

guidelines.rule: Tile-based games MUST use DIFFERENT animation curves for different phases. Bulging = .easeOut(0.08) (fast pop). Shrinking = .easeIn(0.10) (accelerate as it disappears). Landing = .spring(response:0.18, dampingFraction:0.45) (bouncy). Idle restore = .spring(0.20, 0.78) (settle). One global animation curve for everything reads as 'boring spring on everything' — different timings per phase is what separates 'alive' from 'sterile.'
guidelines.why: Real Candy Crush uses different curves per phase (researched 2026-05-04). v3/v4/v5 of catalog Match3 used one global spring; user feedback was 'feels flat.' v6 with per-phase curves was the first one that 'felt like candy.' Universal — applies to any squash-stretch animated UI.
examples: RIGHT: animationFor(.bulging) returns .easeOut(0.08); animationFor(.shrinking) returns .easeIn(0.10); animationFor(.squashing) returns .spring(response:0.18, dampingFraction:0.45). Apply each via .animation(animationFor(phase), value: phase) on the tile.
WRONG: withAnimation(.spring) wrapping the entire match-resolve cycle — every phase rides one curve, feels uniform.

guidelines.rule: Game generators creating tile-based or arcade games SHOULD use the `effects.animation-intensity` Lego component instead of hardcoding squash/bulge/spring magic numbers in ContentView. Pick a preset based on game pace: `.subtle` for productivity-flavored puzzles (crossword/word/sudoku/utility), `.standard` for mechanics-driven games (match-3/minesweeper/breakout), `.punchy` for arcade-action (asteroids/pacman/plants-vs-zombies/kids-games). One preset switch rescales every effect together; individual fields can be overridden for fine-tuning. Stamp the preset choice into a `// AnimationIntensity: subtle` comment near the property declaration so it's scannable.
guidelines.why: Without a shared knob struct, tuning means hunting for magic numbers across the View; copying numbers from one game to another forgets to scale them for pace. AnimationIntensity centralizes the tuning surface. Filed 2026-05-04 after Match3 v6 work + user request 'might almost be nice to define controls that allow us to dial up and down some of this stuff so we can quickly tune a given game or app.'
examples: RIGHT: `private let intensity: AnimationIntensity = .standard  // AnimationIntensity: standard (match-3 = mechanics-driven feedback)` then use `intensity.bulgeAmount`, `intensity.flashScale`, etc. throughout the View.
RIGHT (override individual): `var i = AnimationIntensity.standard; i.flashScale = 0.5; i.flashOpacity = 0.4 // dial flashes back further` and assign to `intensity`.
WRONG: hardcoded `.scaleEffect(1.25)`, `.font(.system(size: 92))`, `.spring(response: 0.18, dampingFraction: 0.45)` scattered across the View — impossible to retune as a unit.

guidelines.rule: Per-event flash overlays (sparkle for 3-match, burst for 4-match, lightning for 5-match — and any equivalent 'event happened here' visual) should be SCALED DOWN and SEMI-TRANSPARENT, not full-size solid graphics. Rule of thumb: glyph size ~60% of cell size, opacity 0.4–0.6, halo radius modest. Overly large/solid flash overlays read as 'sticker pasted on top of the gameplay' rather than 'effect emanating from the gameplay.' Real production overlays are accents, not centerpieces. Avoid giant text labels like '⚡ 5-MATCH ⚡' — the game-mechanic feedback (score increment, board change) is the centerpiece; the flash is punctuation.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-04 on catalog Match3 v6.3: 'the animation with the star and the lightning bolt are a bit too big and should probably be a little more transparent.' v6.4 flashes use scale=0.6 + opacity=0.55 + halo=18, removed text labels — and the flashes still register but no longer dominate. AnimationIntensity captures this as `flashScale: 0.6, flashOpacity: 0.55` in the standard preset.
examples: RIGHT: Image(systemName: "sparkles").font(.system(size: 56 * intensity.flashScale, weight: .black)).foregroundStyle(color.opacity(intensity.flashOpacity)).shadow(color: color.opacity(intensity.flashOpacity * 0.8), radius: intensity.flashHaloRadius)
RIGHT: NO 'YOU DID IT' / '5-MATCH' text labels — the score increment + board change communicates the event.
WRONG: opaque 96pt bolt.fill + 150pt circle + 60pt halo + giant Text('⚡ 5-MATCH ⚡') label centered on top of the board. Reads as 'graphic asset' not 'effect.'

guidelines.rule: Game-flavor mini-apps (arcade, match-3, card, puzzle, board games — anything in the 'game' bucket) SHOULD use the `effects.sound-engine` Lego component instead of building their own AVAudioPlayer plumbing. SoundEngine provides pooled players (low-latency overlap), debounced playback, music fade in/out, persistent UserDefaults mute, semantic SFX enum (match3Pop / arcadeLaser / cardSlide / etc.), and intensity presets (.subtle/.standard/.punchy/.soft). 25 CC0 SFX from Kenney are bundled in `assets/sfx/*.m4a`. Generated apps should declare `@ObservedObject private var sound = SoundEngine.shared` and call `sound.play(.match3Pop)`. Add `resources: [.copy("SFX")]` to the SwiftPM target.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 after building SoundEngine component for catalog Match3 v7. User feedback: 'sound effects really add to the overall game experience.' Without a shared engine, every game reinvents pool management, debouncing, mute toggle, music fades — and gets 2-3 of them wrong (jarring volumes, no fade, plays on every render).
examples: RIGHT: import effects.sound-engine; @ObservedObject private var sound = SoundEngine.shared; on match-clear: sound.play(.match3Pop, pitchSemitones: cascadeDepth * 2)
RIGHT (mute button): Button { sound.muted.toggle() } label: { Image(systemName: sound.muted ? "speaker.slash.fill" : "speaker.wave.2.fill") } — required by audio-always-mute-toggle rule.
WRONG: build AVAudioPlayer pool ad-hoc in ContentView; reinvent debouncing; forget mute persistence; skip music fades.

guidelines.rule: Any game with sound MUST expose a visible, persistent mute toggle in the always-visible UI (not hidden in a settings menu). Default state: unmuted at modest volume (intensity.standard.sfxVolume = 0.65). Mute state MUST persist across app restarts via UserDefaults. Use SF Symbols `speaker.wave.2.fill` (unmuted) / `speaker.slash.fill` (muted). Optional but recommended: bind to keyboard shortcut 'M'. SoundEngine.muted handles the persistence — just observe `sound.muted` for the icon swap.
guidelines.why: Universal UX rule. Users tab between games, take calls, share screens. Burying mute deep in a settings menu means the next time they alt-tab in, the SFX surprise everyone in the room. Filed 2026-05-04 alongside SoundEngine ship — incorporated as default behavior in the engine itself but the visible-button is a View-level requirement that can't be enforced by the Lego.
examples: RIGHT: speaker icon in the bottom controls bar, always visible. Tap → mute persists.
RIGHT: keyboardShortcut("m", modifiers: []) for power-users + .help() tooltip.
WRONG: mute hidden in a 'Preferences' menu / hamburger / kebab. Surfacing-cost too high.

guidelines.rule: For match-3 / cascade / chain-reaction games, pitch-shift the SFX +2 semitones per cascade depth (depth 0 = neutral, depth 1 = +2, depth 2 = +4, etc.). Creates the satisfying ascending arpeggio Candy Crush is famous for. Use `sound.play(sfx, pitchSemitones: depth * 2)`. SoundEngine supports this via `enableRate = true` on the underlying AVAudioPlayer pool. Cap at depth 6 (= +12 semitones = double pitch) — beyond that AVAudioPlayer artifacts become obvious. Apply same pattern to: combo counters in arcade games (each combo bumps pitch), enemy-streak-kills, multi-line-clear in Tetris.
guidelines.why: Real Candy Crush production behavior validated 2026-05-04 via web research + Match3 v7 implementation. Without pitch-shift, every cascade pop sounds identical → user can't tell they're chaining. With pitch-shift, the ear hears 'thing 1, thing 2, thing 3' as ascending tones and the cascade feels satisfying. Universal — applies to any game with chainable events.
examples: RIGHT: sound.play(.match3Pop, pitchSemitones: cascadeDepth * 2) — direct swap = neutral, first cascade = +2, second = +4, etc.
RIGHT (combos): sound.play(.arcadeExplosion, pitchSemitones: min(comboCount, 6) * 2)
WRONG: same flat tone on every cascade pop — user can't tell they're chaining.

guidelines.rule: Sound effects must fire on DISCRETE GAME EVENTS (match resolved, button tapped, enemy hit, level up), NOT inside SwiftUI render or `body` evaluation. SoundEngine debounces internally (30ms window per SFX), but if you call `sound.play(.x)` from a `.onAppear` / `body` getter / @State observer that fires on every render, you'll hammer the engine — and the debounce will silently drop most of them but the ones that DO play will be misaligned with what the user did. Place SFX calls inside the action handlers: `Button(action: { sound.play(.uiButtonTap); ...real action... })` or inside the engine-event loop where matches resolve.
guidelines.why: Same anti-pattern as 'wrote tile content into ForEach Text() body' — render-time side effects = unpredictable. Filed 2026-05-04 after wiring Match3: original instinct was to call sound.play(.match3Pop) inside the `flashView` ViewBuilder. That would have fired on every flash render, not just when the flash was created. Moved into the cascade-loop where events.kind is known once per resolution.
examples: RIGHT: in the cascade loop where match events resolve, exactly once per event: `for event in previewEvents { sound.play(matchSFX(for: event.kind), pitchSemitones: event.cascadeDepth * 2) }`
WRONG: `var body: some View { Text("\(score)").onAppear { sound.play(.scoreTick) } }` — fires every render.

WRONG: `.onChange(of: engine.score) { sound.play(.coinPickup) }` triggers on EVERY score change including the +30 from a 3-match → could fire 6+ times in a single cascade. Either debounce upstream or fire on the discrete match event, not the score derivative.

guidelines.rule: Same pattern as AnimationIntensity: pick a SoundIntensity preset by game pace. `.subtle` (sfxVol=0.35, musicVol=0.15) for productivity-flavored puzzles where audio is light punctuation. `.standard` (0.65/0.35) for mechanics-driven games (match-3, minesweeper). `.punchy` (0.85/0.55) for arcade-action (asteroids, pacman, plants-vs-zombies, kids/casual). `.soft` (0.5/0.45) for relaxing/idle/ambient (solitaire, pattern matchers). Stamp the choice in a comment near the SoundEngine declaration: `// SoundIntensity: standard (match-3 mechanics-driven feedback)`.
guidelines.why: Without a shared knob struct, generators copy-paste a volume float and forget to scale for pace. Match-3 at .punchy is too loud; arcade at .subtle disappears. Presets capture the calibration. Mirrors the AnimationIntensity pattern intentionally so generators learn one mental model.
examples: RIGHT: SoundEngine.shared.intensity = .standard  // SoundIntensity: standard (match-3)
RIGHT (override): var i = SoundIntensity.standard; i.sfxVolume = 0.5; i.musicVolume = 0.25; SoundEngine.shared.intensity = i
WRONG: hardcoded `sound.play(.match3Pop, volume: 0.7)` everywhere — impossible to retune as a unit.

guidelines.rule: Background music tracks MUST fade in (1.0–1.5s) and fade out (1.0s) — NEVER hard-cut. Hard cuts are jarring and feel like a glitch. SoundEngine.loop(_:fadeIn:) handles this: `sound.loop(.puzzleAmbient, fadeIn: 1.5)`. Track switches: stopMusic(fadeOut: 0.3) THEN loop(.next, fadeIn: 1.0) — for a clean crossfade-via-stop. App-quit / sheet-dismiss / scene-change: stopMusic(fadeOut: 1.0) before transition. SFX are exempt — they're already short, fade is built into the sound itself.
guidelines.why: Universal audio production rule. Hard cuts on long-form audio = listener notices the seam = breaks immersion. Filed 2026-05-04 with SoundEngine ship — the engine enforces fade defaults so hard cuts only happen if a generator explicitly passes 0.
examples: RIGHT: sound.loop(.puzzleAmbient, fadeIn: 1.5) on game start; sound.stopMusic(fadeOut: 1.0) on game-over screen.
WRONG: musicPlayer.play() / musicPlayer.stop() without fade. Listener hears the abrupt cut.

guidelines.rule: Progression-flavored games (match-3, arcade-with-waves, puzzles-with-difficulty-tiers, runners, idle clickers, platformers with progressing stages) MUST use the `effects.level-manager` Lego component instead of inventing target-score / progression math from scratch. LevelManager provides: difficulty curves (.casual 1.5× / .standard 1.4× / .hardcore 1.3× / .accelerating / .custom), per-level moves budget, suggestedPaletteSize / suggestedSpawnRateMultiplier / suggestedAnimationIntensity helpers, persistent currentLevel via UserDefaults, and a celebration window (`celebratingLevelUp` / `celebrationProgress`) for the banner + SFX. Wire it as `@StateObject private var levels = LevelManager(gameID: "<game>", baseTarget: <num>, baseMovesAllowed: <num>, curve: .standard)`.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 alongside LevelManager ship. Without a shared component, every progression game writes ad-hoc 'targetScore *= 1.5' arithmetic, forgets to persist current level, and has no celebration moment — users finish level 1, see the same screen, and lose engagement. LevelManager + the celebration window turn 'beat target' into a reward.
examples: RIGHT: @StateObject private var levels = LevelManager(gameID: "match3", baseTarget: 1500, baseMovesAllowed: 30, curve: .standard); HUD reads `Text("LEVEL \(levels.currentLevel)")` and `Text("\(levels.currentTarget)")`. On target hit: `levels.advance()`.
RIGHT (UI): scaleEffect on level pill = `1.0 + levels.celebrationProgress * 0.20`; level-up banner opacity = `Double(levels.celebrationProgress)`.
RIGHT (palette growth): `engine.symbols = Array(["A","B","C","D","E","F","G","H"].prefix(levels.suggestedPaletteSize))` — palette starts at 5 colors, grows by +1 every 5 levels, capped at 8.
WRONG: hardcoded `private let targetScore = 1500`, no progression. User beats it, sees nothing change, exits app, never returns.
WRONG: ad-hoc `if score >= 1500 { score = 0; targetScore = 2500 }` — no curve, no persistence, no celebration.

guidelines.rule: When a player advances a level, the UI MUST acknowledge it with a 1-3 second celebration: (a) a prominent 'LEVEL N' banner that inflates and fades, (b) a level-up SFX (SoundEngine.play(.levelUp) — pitch-shift +2 semitones for stronger effect), (c) a brief scale/glow pulse on the level pill / score readout, (d) a tactile pause (~0.5–1s) before the new board / wave is presented. Skipping the celebration means the player isn't told they accomplished something — and the dopamine hit of progression is the entire reason to play. LevelManager provides `celebratingLevelUp: Bool` and `celebrationProgress: CGFloat` (0→1→0 across the window) — bind animations to these.
guidelines.why: Universal game-design principle. Filed 2026-05-04 with LevelManager ship. Without celebration, a level-up feels like a level-down — the user hits the target and the game just changes the number. With celebration (banner + SFX + pulse), the user FEELS the win. Match3 v8 implementation: level pill scaleEffect(1.0 + celebrationProgress * 0.20), banner Text scaleEffect(0.7 + celebrationProgress * 0.6) + opacity(celebrationProgress), levelUp SFX with +2 pitch.
examples: RIGHT: levels.advance() triggers celebrationProgress ramp 0→1 over 0.5s, plateau 0.5s, 1→0 over 2s. View binds banner opacity/scale to celebrationProgress; SFX plays at the start.
RIGHT (audio sequence): on target hit → sound.play(.victory) → 450ms pause → sound.play(.levelUp, pitchSemitones: 2) → levels.advance() triggers banner.
WRONG: levels.advance() called silently with no UI reaction. Player isn't sure if anything happened.
WRONG: instant snap-cut to next level (no banner, no SFX, no delay). Feels like a glitch.

guidelines.rule: When a player fails a level (out of moves, lives depleted, time up), the game MUST allow replay of the SAME level — never auto-demote to a lower level or back to L1. Save the player's progression at all times. RESTART buttons should reset score/moves WITHIN the current level, not bump levels.currentLevel down. Players who hit a hard level will replay it many times; demoting them would be a punishment that destroys engagement. The only way to drop a level should be a deliberate user action (e.g. a 'Reset Progress' settings toggle, never the in-game RESTART button).
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 alongside LevelManager. Hardcoded into LevelManager API: `replayCurrentLevel()` is a no-op on persistence; only `reset()` (explicit reset-progress action) resets currentLevel to 1. Universal UX rule — applied incorrectly, players quit the game forever after one rage-fail.
examples: RIGHT: RESTART button rebuilds engine, refreshes moves to levels.currentMovesAllowed, but does NOT touch levels.currentLevel.
RIGHT: 'Reset Progress' in settings menu calls levels.reset() — opt-in only.
WRONG: RESTART resets currentLevel to 1. Player who beat 5 levels and rage-quit on L6 has to redo all 5.

guidelines.rule: Higher levels should feel different across MULTIPLE dimensions, not just 'higher target number.' For tile/match games: grow the symbol palette (LevelManager.suggestedPaletteSize: 5→6 at L5, 7 at L10, 8 at L15), shrink moves budget if pacing demands it (.accelerating curve), bump animation intensity (LevelManager.suggestedAnimationIntensity: 'subtle' L1-4 → 'standard' L5-9 → 'punchy' L10+). For arcade games: spawn-rate (LevelManager.suggestedSpawnRateMultiplier: 1.0× → 3.0× capped at L20+), enemy variety, projectile speed, intermittent boss waves at L5/L10/L15. The TARGET getting larger is just one knob — alone, it produces 'same game, takes longer.' Multi-knob progression produces 'this level FEELS different.'
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 with LevelManager ship. Real Candy Crush / Bejeweled / etc. grow many things per level (board shape, special-tile generation rate, blockers, mission types). Reference apps in the catalog should at minimum grow palette + intensity for tile games, spawn-rate + intensity for arcade games. LevelManager exposes the suggested helpers so generators don't have to invent them.
examples: RIGHT (match-3): L1: 5 colors, .standard intensity. L5: 6 colors, .standard. L10: 7 colors, .punchy. L15: 8 colors, .punchy. Each level feels harder for a NEW reason.
RIGHT (arcade): L1: 2 enemies, slow projectiles, .standard intensity. L5: 4 enemies, faster projectiles, .standard. L10: 6 enemies + occasional bomb-spawn, .punchy.
WRONG: every level identical except target=basicTarget*1.4^N. Player perceives 'same game just slower to finish' — no surprise, no novelty, drops off.

guidelines.rule: Choose LevelCurve by intended play session length: `.casual` (1.5× per level, fast ramp) for short kid-friendly sessions where players should reach high levels in 10-15 minutes. `.standard` (1.4×) for mechanics-driven games (match-3, minesweeper) where 30-60 min sessions are typical. `.hardcore` (1.3×, slow ramp) for endurance/idle/score-attack games designed for long-term engagement. `.accelerating` (gentle 1.3× early, climbs to 1.6× by L20+) for games that should onboard easily but become a real challenge — typical for puzzle franchises. `.custom` for games with non-monotonic difficulty (boss levels, breather levels, jumps).
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04. Same generator-mental-model pattern as AnimationIntensity / SoundIntensity — pick a preset by game pace, not by inventing arithmetic. Wrong curve = engagement disaster: hardcore curve on a casual game = players quit at L4; casual curve on hardcore = players hit L99 in an hour and have nothing left.
examples: RIGHT: LevelManager(gameID: "kid-game", curve: .casual) — fast progression, frequent level-up celebrations.
RIGHT: LevelManager(gameID: "match3", curve: .standard) — balanced.
RIGHT: LevelManager(gameID: "score-attack", curve: .hardcore) — slow ramp.
WRONG: pick a curve based on coin flip / didn't think about pace; ship a casual-curve match-3 (player ceiling-hits at L20) or hardcore-curve casual game (player gives up at L4).

guidelines.rule: Persist `currentLevel` (the structural progression) via UserDefaults. Do NOT persist `currentScore` (the within-level progress) — it should reset on app launch. Players don't expect to resume a partially-played level mid-game; they expect to come back, see their level number, and start that level fresh. LevelManager handles the level persistence under key 'LevelManager.<gameID>.currentLevel'. SoundEngine.muted is also persisted (separately). Anything else (current score, board state, current cascade depth, hint pair) is ephemeral.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 with LevelManager. Saving mid-level state is high-effort + low-value: requires Codable engine state, can desync if engine impl changes, players have to mentally context-switch back into a half-finished puzzle. Saving level-only is what users expect from casual-game UX.
examples: RIGHT: LevelManager persists currentLevel automatically. App launches → onAppear sets engine.symbols based on suggestedPaletteSize, moves = currentMovesAllowed, status = 'Level N — target M'. Score starts at 0.
WRONG: persist current Match3Engine state across launches. Brittle, annoying for users, breaks when engine model evolves.

guidelines.rule: Personal best / high-score should be surfaced in the GAME-OVER (or run-summary) panel, NOT in the always-visible in-play HUD. During active play, in-play high-score chrome (BEST pills, live 'X to beat' / '+N ahead' deltas) is visual noise that distracts from the actual gameplay — players are mid-decision and don't want a constant comparison ticker. The end-of-run panel is when the comparison is meaningful: it's a single moment, the player has earned their score, and seeing 'Best: 12,450' alongside 'Your run: 11,200' creates the natural motivation to play again. Use HighScoreManager.commit(score:) at game-over → it returns true on a new best, fires celebrationProgress for a 'NEW BEST!' flourish on the panel. The in-play HUD shows ONLY the active counters (score / level / moves / time).
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04. Initial implementation v9 put BEST + live delta + 'NEW BEST!' chrome in the always-visible HUD. User feedback: 'why did you go so overboard with the highscore thing? this is a super ugly header. have you seen a high score on a game, it shouldn't show up until the game is done so the user doesn't get so distracted.' Established games (Candy Crush, Bejeweled, Threes, every console arcade game) put high score in the game-over screen — never as constant in-play chrome. v9.1 moved it to a modal that appears only at moves==0.
examples: RIGHT: in-play HUD = SCORE | LEVEL | MOVES (just active counters). Game-over panel = OUT OF MOVES / NEW BEST! header, big run-total readout, 'Best: 12,450' line with trophy icon, PLAY AGAIN button.
RIGHT: highScores.commit(totalScore) at moves==0 OR game-end → returns true on new best → panel header flips to 'NEW BEST!' with gold styling.
WRONG: BEST pill always visible in the HUD with live 'N to beat' / '+N ahead' delta during play. Distracts from the gameplay decision.
WRONG: 'NEW BEST!' celebration that fires DURING play (e.g. mid-cascade). Save it for the game-over moment so it's a clean reward.

guidelines.rule: Multi-level games MUST track BOTH a cumulative score (carries across levels in the session) AND a per-level score (zeros each level for progress-toward-target). Surface BOTH in the HUD: TOTAL = cumulative session score (this is what you compare against personal best); LEVEL SCORE = this level's progress, drives the level-progress bar / star thresholds / level-up trigger. Wiring: `totalScore = baseTotalForLevel + engine.score`. On level-up: `baseTotalForLevel = totalScore` (anchor the new level's floor), then re-seed engine (engine.score back to 0). Without two-tier scores, players who beat 5 levels see 'their score' reset on level transitions and lose the sense of session progress.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 after user feedback 'after going to a new level my score gets reset to 0 instead of setting some additional point goal.' Same conceptual error as level-replay-not-demote — UX should never feel like progress was wiped. Match-3 / RPG / arcade-with-waves / endless-mode all benefit. Two-score model is also what HighScoreManager.commit(score:) should be called with — the cumulative TOTAL, not the per-level score.
examples: RIGHT: HUD shows TOTAL: 12,450 prominently; LEVEL SCORE: 320 / 3,000 below it. On level-up, TOTAL stays at 12,450 + earnings; LEVEL SCORE flips to 0 / 4,200 for the new level.
RIGHT: highScores.commit(score: totalScore) — commits cumulative session total, not per-level.
WRONG: only show engine.score, which resets every level. User thinks they're starting over each time.
WRONG: commit per-level score to HighScoreManager — your 'best' becomes 'highest single level' instead of 'longest run.'

guidelines.rule: Mutable in-game counters (moves remaining, lives, ammo, time) MUST be guarded by `max(0, …)` and gated at their entry points. A `moves -= 1` followed by a refund-on-fail pattern can race or compound — multiple events firing close together can push the counter negative. Pattern: `guard moves > 0 else { /* show out-of-moves UI */; return }` BEFORE the action, then `moves = max(0, moves - 1)` only after the action commits successfully. Failed actions (bad swap / missed shot / etc.) should NOT charge the counter — players punished for mis-timing input churn out fast.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 after user reported 'somehow I managed to get to -1 moves' in catalog Match3 v8. Bug was: `moves -= 1` ran before the swap-success check; refund only happened on the mis-input path. Fixed with explicit guard at entry + max(0,…) on the decrement + don't-charge-bad-swaps. Universal pattern for any decrementing counter.
examples: RIGHT: guard moves > 0 else { status = "Out of moves — RESTART to retry"; sound.play(.uiError); return }; ... if !didSwap { sound.play(.uiError); return /* don't charge */ }; moves = max(0, moves - 1)
WRONG: moves -= 1; if !didSwap { moves += 1; return }  — racy under rapid input, can bottom out below 0.
WRONG: charge a move on failed actions. Player who mis-drags loses count for nothing.

guidelines.rule: Game engines that run `resolveCascades()` / equivalent setup logic during init MUST explicitly zero the score at the end of init. Initial-fill cascades produce score increments that are NOT user-earned. Without explicit zeroing, fresh games + level-up board re-seeds start at 200-1000+ ghost score depending on seed luck. Pattern in init: `_ = resolveCascades(); lastResolvedMatches = []; score = 0`. Engine.swap() / engine.advance() — the user-driven entry points — are the only paths that should award score.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 from real bug in catalog Match3Engine. init() called resolveCascades() to clear initial-fill matches before showing the board, which awarded score for each cascade. Only the event log was cleared, not the score. User report: 'my score never starts at 0.' Same pattern applies to Tetris (initial cleared rows during piece-spawn-warmup), RPG (XP from seed-creation enemies), any game with auto-resolve in init.
examples: RIGHT: init() { ... refillAll(); _ = resolveCascades(); lastResolvedMatches = []; score = 0 }
WRONG: init() runs resolveCascades() (which awards score), then only clears event log — fresh games start at 200-1000 'ghost' points.

guidelines.rule: Score readouts ≥1000 MUST use thousands separators ('12,450' not '12450'; '1,234,567' not '1234567'). Numeric readability drops sharply past 4 digits without separators. HighScoreManager.formatNumber(_:) provides the canonical formatter (uses NumberFormatter with .decimal style); use it for ALL score-like numbers (current score, best score, target, total, delta). Targets that are still 4-digit (like '1500') can be unformatted but consistency is better — format everything once you cross the threshold.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 with HighScoreManager ship. Universal readability rule. '125000' is a wall of digits; '125,000' is instantly parseable. Costs nothing, consistency is high-value.
examples: RIGHT: Text(HighScoreManager.formatNumber(totalScore)) → '12,450'
RIGHT: Text("+\(HighScoreManager.formatNumber(delta)) ahead") → '+1,200 ahead'
WRONG: Text("\(totalScore)") → '12450' (no separator)

guidelines.rule: Game state transitions (level-up, game-over, round-restart, board reseed) MUST be SYNCHRONOUS and ATOMIC. Do NOT split state mutation across `Task { try await Task.sleep(...) }` segments where the visual celebration runs in parallel with state-resetting code. While the user waits for sleeps to complete, they can swap / fire / tap, and the next render-driven turn reads HALF-MUTATED state — causing a cluster of bugs: score resets to 0 (new turn reads stale baseTotalForLevel), counters don't refresh (UI bound to var that hasn't been reassigned yet), RESTART takes you to the wrong level (level-number advanced but engine hasn't been swapped). The fix: do all state mutation in ONE turn (typically inside one `withAnimation` block), and run the celebration banner via a parallel @Published value (e.g. LevelManager.celebrationProgress) that the View animates from independently. SFX is fine to fire as a delayed Task — audio-only, no state mutation, can't race.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 from Match3 v9 → v9.2 incident. v9 split level-up across two `try await Task.sleep` segments (450ms + 650ms) with state-resets happening AFTER the sleeps. User reported three distinct bugs that were all the same race: 'my score went back to 0 at a certain level' (next cascade read stale baseTotalForLevel), 'moves didn't reset after some levels' (UI bound to moves var that hadn't been reassigned yet), 'RESTART only takes me back to the previous level' (RESTART read half-mutated state — level number advanced but engine not yet swapped). v9.2 made the entire transition atomic in one withAnimation block; banner animation runs from levels.celebrationProgress which advances on its own.
examples: RIGHT (atomic): when level target hit → run all in one turn: `levels.advance(); engine = newEngine; moves = levels.currentMovesAllowed; baseTotalForLevel = totalScore; victoryAnnounced = false`. The banner animates from levels.celebrationProgress (a @Published 0→1→0 value the controller advances internally). SFX fires as a one-shot Task — audio only.

RIGHT (visual delay decoupled from state): celebration banner reads `if levels.celebratingLevelUp { ... .opacity(Double(levels.celebrationProgress)) }`. Banner can take 3 seconds to fade out — gameplay state is already consistent at frame 1.
WRONG: `Task { try await Task.sleep(450ms); levels.advance(); try await Task.sleep(650ms); engine = newEngine; moves = newMoves }`. During the sleeps, user input lands on stale state.

WRONG: setting `victoryAnnounced = false` inside an async withAnimation that runs LATER than other state mutations — gives the next turn's runCascadeLoop() permission to re-fire the win condition before the engine has been swapped.
Acceptable async (audio only): `Task { try await Task.sleep(350ms); sound.play(.levelUp) }` — no state mutation, no race possible.

guidelines.rule: Cribbage starter card (the 'cut') MUST NOT be visible during the DISCARD phase. The starter is cut from the deck AFTER both players discard 2 cards to the crib — its early reveal lets the player strategize their discards using forbidden information, breaking the game's strategic core. Render the cut-card slot empty (or as a face-down card back) during phase='discard'. Reveal the starter face-up ONLY when phase transitions to 'pegging' (or 'cut-reveal' if you split the phase). If the starter is a Jack, award 'his heels' (2 points) to the DEALER at that reveal moment, not during discard.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-11: 'cut card is revealed too early (I can strategize before I put into the crib)'. Cribbage strategy hinges on the player NOT knowing the starter when they discard — knowing the starter would let the player keep an optimal hand against that exact card. Showing it early is a critical correctness bug, not a UX nit.
examples: Phase guard: if (G.phase === 'discard') renderStarterSlot('face-down')
Reveal trigger: after doDiscard() completes, call revealStarter() then startPegging()
Animation: brief fade-in or flip animation on the starter slot so the reveal moment is felt
DO NOT: render starter face-up in the same frame as the discard buttons appear

guidelines.rule: Cribbage layout MUST be compact and viewport-aware: max-width 480px on the play surface, content centered with NO 30%+ blank green space in the middle of the viewport. Use CSS flex with `flex: 1` only on a single mid-zone (the play area / starter row), not multiple competing regions. On mobile (<500px), the entire game state (board strip, CPU hand, mid-zone, player hand, action button) MUST fit in 100dvh with no scroll. Cards MUST be at least 56×80px on desktop and 44×64px on mobile so the rank+suit are legible at arm's length. Player-hand cards should overlap (margin-right: -8px to -12px) like real cards in a fan, not be widely spaced.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-11: 'There is far too much green open space on desktop and this does not fit comfortably on mobile. The cards look a little weird and cards could be larger.' Previous generations centered a narrow board strip inside a 1000+px-tall viewport leaving most of the screen empty, AND shipped 32px cards that are unreadable at desktop viewing distance.
examples: Top-level: body { display: flex; flex-direction: column; height: 100dvh; max-width: 480px; margin: 0 auto; }
Mid-zone: .mid-zone { flex: 1; display: flex; flex-direction: column; align-items: center; justify-content: center; min-height: 0; }
Cards desktop: .card { width: 56px; height: 80px; } — on mobile (max-width: 500px): .card { width: 44px; height: 64px; }
Hand overlap: .hand-row .card { margin-right: -10px; } and .card:last-child { margin-right: 0; }
DO NOT: ship a slim 22px board-strip + 30% empty mid-zone + cramped 32px cards

guidelines.rule:
Reuse the existing platform card-rendering pattern. Playing cards in any card game (cribbage, blackjack, hearts, etc.) MUST follow the canonical mChatAI card style established by cg-012: WHITE card face, BLACK text for ♠♣, RED text for ♥♦. Rank appears top-left and bottom-right (bottom-right rotated 180°), suit symbol centered and larger than the rank. Card.js (entities/Card.js) is the canonical DATA model — use its `rank`, `suit`, `color` getters when modeling the game state. For rendering, follow the markup pattern:
{{R}}{{♠♥♦♣}}{{R}}
. Do NOT invent novel card styling (emoji faces, gradient suits, decorative borders) — players need cards that read INSTANTLY as playing cards.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-11: 'The cards look a little weird... I thought we already have cards we've used in other games...' The platform has had recurring complaints about generated cards looking weird (smiley faces baked into court cards, gradient suits, etc.). Standard playing-card aesthetics are universally recognized; deviation hurts readability and signals 'AI-generated' to players.
examples:
Markup:
5♥5
Styling: .card { background: #fff; border: 1.5px solid #ccc; border-radius: 6px; padding: 4px; } .card.red { color: #dc2626; } .card.black { color: #111; }
Data: import { Card } from '<webcomponents>/entities/Card.js'; const c = new Card('5', 'hearts');
DO NOT: render Jack/Queen/King as emoji smiley faces or images — use the letter (J/Q/K) with the suit symbol like all other ranks

guidelines.rule: After a scoring sub-round closes (cribbage: hitting 31, a Go, the last card played; trick games like hearts/spades/whist: a completed trick; blackjack: a dealer reveal; any moment where points are awarded and the table is about to clear), the game MUST give the player time to absorb what happened before resetting. Use ONE of: (a) an explicit 'Continue' button the player clicks to advance — preferred, deterministic, respects both slow and fast players; (b) a fixed pause of at least 5 seconds during which the final pile/score/highlight remains visible and the player CANNOT auto-advance past the moment. Do NOT chain CPU plays through setTimeout into an immediate reset/clear of the play area — the player will see cards flicker into a pile and then vanish before they can read the score or recognize the combination (e.g., 'wait, was that a 15-2 pair-2 for 4?'). The Continue button SHOULD be on the same affordance band as the existing action buttons (Play / Go) so the player's hand doesn't have to wander; the message accompanying it MUST name (i) WHO scored, (ii) WHAT they scored, and (iii) WHY (e.g., 'CPU hit 31 — round resets to 0', 'You scored Go for being last to play', 'Last card — on to The Show'). The same pattern applies to trick games (after a trick is taken, hold the trick visible until the player advances or 5s elapse) and to any turn-based game where the CPU produces points faster than a human can read.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-18: 'after a CPU turn goes and it is the end of a round, allow the player at least 5 seconds (or some kind of button the player needs to hit to continue) so the player can evaluate the final hand and go oh yeah that's what that is rather than wondering what just happened, or a hand that ends in 31 or a go or whatever. I've noticed the same issue with other card games so there might be a universal rule here.' Without this pause, scoring events feel arbitrary — the player sees points appear on the board but cannot tie them to the cards that produced them, which erodes trust in the rules engine and removes the strategic learning loop.
examples: Cribbage pegging: count reaches 31 → keep pile + count visible, show 'CPU hit 31 — round resets to 0  Continue ▸' button, ONLY clear the pile when the button is clicked.
Cribbage Go: both players can't play → award Go point, keep pile visible, show 'You scored Go (last to play). Round resets to 0  Continue ▸' button.
Hearts trick taken: 4th card laid → keep the trick visible (do not animate cards into the taker's pile) until the player clicks Continue OR ≥5s elapses.
RIGHT (deterministic): controls.innerHTML=''; controls.appendChild(continueBtn); continueBtn.onclick = () => { pegPile=[]; pegCount=0; resetRound(); };
WRONG: setTimeout(() => { pegPile=[]; pegCount=0; renderHands(); cpuPlay(); }, 800); — the player has no time to absorb the 31, and on a fast machine 800ms looks like a glitch.
